title: Dog Ate Xylitol Gum: Urgent Help & Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate gum with xylitol, act fast. Xylitol is highly toxic to dogs. Follow this guide for emergency steps and veterinary triage info.
content_html:
What is Xylitol?

Xylitol is a common sugar substitute found in many sugar-free products, including chewing gum, breath mints, and some peanut butters. While harmless to humans, it is a potent toxin for dogs because it triggers a rapid, massive release of insulin from their pancreas.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. This is a life-threatening emergency with a high urgency level. Ingestion of xylitol can cause a sudden, dangerous drop in blood sugar (hypoglycemia) and can lead to acute liver failure or even death if not treated immediately by a professional.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If possible, take a clear photo of the gum's packaging, specifically the ingredient list and the weight of the product. This information is vital because different brands contain varying concentrations of xylitol. Having a photo allows your veterinarian to calculate the exact toxic dose your dog may have consumed and prioritize treatment accordingly.

faqs.question: How quickly do symptoms of xylitol poisoning appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms such as vomiting, loss of coordination, or lethargy can appear as quickly as 15 to 30 minutes after ingestion.

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if my dog acts sick?
faqs.answer: No. Waiting for symptoms is dangerous because liver damage or severe hypoglycemia can occur before you notice outward signs.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not attempt to induce vomiting unless explicitly directed by a veterinarian, as this can be dangerous if your dog is already becoming hypoglycemic.
